children.name: Rhipidura
children.description: The fantails — a large genus of small, active insectivores named for their long, fanned tails that are constantly in motion, flicked, cocked, and spread in a characteristic display. They are found from South Asia through Australasia to the Pacific islands. Their restless foraging behaviour involves continuous movement through the understorey, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Rhipidura leucophrys
children.description: Australia's most familiar fantail, a boldly patterned black-and-white bird with a long, constantly wagging tail, found across Australia, New Guinea, and the Solomon Islands. It is bold and confiding, often approaching humans closely in gardens and parks. Its aggressive, chattering calls are directed at intruders far larger than itself, and it is one of the few small birds that will actively mob raptors.

children.name: Rhipidura albiscapa
children.description: A small, active grey fantail with a prominent white eyebrow and a long, fanned tail that it constantly spreads and flicks while foraging, found across Australia, New Caledonia, and the Solomon Islands. It is a hyperactive insectivore that twists and turns through the air in pursuit of prey, fanning its tail at every change of direction. Its thin, piping song is a constant background sound in Australian forests.

children.name: Rhipidura fuliginosa
children.description: One of New Zealand's most beloved birds, a small fantail with a broadly fanned tail that is dark above and white-tipped, found in both the North and South Islands. It is famous for its charming, approachable nature, often fluttering close to humans in forests and gardens. Its constant, acrobatic foraging and cheerful 'cheep-cheep' call make it a favourite among New Zealand birdwatchers.

children.name: Rhipidura rufifrons
children.description: A strikingly coloured fantail with a rufous rump, tail base, and forehead, a white throat, and a black-and-white breast band, found in forests of eastern Australia and Pacific islands. It is a restless, lissom forager that fans its tail almost constantly while flitting through the understorey. It is a long-distance migrant within Australia, moving northward in winter.

children.name: Rhipidura rufiventris
children.description: A warm grey-brown fantail with a rufous belly and a pale throat, found in northern Australia, New Guinea, and the Lesser Sundas. It inhabits mangroves, monsoon forests, and woodlands, foraging actively in the lower and mid-storey. Its song is a series of simple, thin, descending whistles.

children.name: Rhipidura aureola
children.description: A handsome fantail with a bold white eyebrow, white throat, and a broad white band across the tail, found in dry forests and scrublands across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It is an active, restless forager that constantly spreads its tail and flits between low perches. Its song is a series of thin, squeaky notes.

children.name: Rhipidura albicollis
children.description: A dark grey fantail with a prominent white throat and white spots on the tail, found in forests from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to southern China. It fans its tail constantly while foraging in the understorey, often in mixed flocks. Its song is a series of sweet, tinkling notes.

children.name: Rhipidura albogularis
children.description: A small fantail with a white throat, a spotted breast, and a long, white-tipped tail, found in forests of central and southern India. It is a hyperactive forager that moves through the lower storey with its tail constantly cocked and spread. Its thin, insect-like calls betray its presence in the dense undergrowth.

children.name: Rhipidura javanica
children.description: A black-and-white fantail with a broad white eyebrow, white wing-bar, and white-tipped tail, found in mangroves, forests, and gardens from Myanmar through Southeast Asia to Borneo. It is a conspicuous and confiding bird, often seen in urban parks. Its tail is in almost constant motion, fanning and flicking as it forages.

children.name: Rhipidura nigritorquis
children.description: A distinctive pied fantail endemic to the Philippines, with a black throat and breast band contrasting with white underparts and a white eyebrow. It is common in forests, second growth, and gardens across most of the archipelago. Its active, fanned-tail foraging style and sharp, scolding calls are a familiar presence in Philippine woodlands.

children.name: Rhipidura nigrorufa
children.description: A striking fantail with black upperparts and a deep rufous breast and belly, endemic to the montane forests of the Western Ghats in India. It fans its black-and-rufous tail constantly as it forages in the understorey. Its thin, high-pitched calls are a characteristic sound of the misty shola forests.

children.name: Rhipidura rufidorsa
children.description: A small fantail with a rufous back, grey head, and white underparts, found in New Guinea and neighbouring islands. It inhabits lowland forests and secondary growth, foraging actively in the mid-storey with constant tail-fanning. Its distribution spans both sides of the Wallace Line.

children.name: Rhipidura superciliaris
children.description: A beautiful fantail with slate-blue upperparts, a pale eyebrow, and white underparts, endemic to the Philippines. It inhabits montane and lowland forests across several islands. It is an active, acrobatic forager that gleans insects from foliage in the mid-storey, constantly fanning its long tail.

children.name: Rhipidura cinereiventris
children.description: A grey fantail found in the Bismarck Archipelago.

children.name: Rhipidura dryas
children.description: A fantail found in the Arafura region, including southern New Guinea and northern Australia.

children.name: Rhipidura euryura
children.description: A white-bellied fantail endemic to the montane forests of Java.

children.name: Rhipidura hypoxantha
children.description: A fantail with yellow underparts, found in the Himalayas and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Rhipidura lepida
children.description: A fantail endemic to Palau in Micronesia.

children.name: Rhipidura leucothorax
children.description: A white-breasted fantail found in New Guinea.

children.name: Rhipidura lorentzi
children.description: A black fantail found in the montane forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Rhipidura malaitae
children.description: A fantail endemic to Malaita in the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Rhipidura matthiae
children.description: A fantail endemic to the Bismarck Archipelago.

children.name: Rhipidura nebulosa
children.description: A fantail endemic to the Samoan Islands.

children.name: Rhipidura nigrocinnerea
children.description: A black-and-cinnamon fantail found in New Guinea.

children.name: Rhipidura opistherista
children.description: A fantail with a very long tail, found in the Moluccas.

children.name: Rhipidura pendrognatha
children.description: A fantail that creeps along branches, found in New Guinea.

children.name: Rhipidura perlata
children.description: A spotted fantail found in the lowland forests of Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula.

children.name: Rhipidura phaea
children.description: A fantail endemic to the Bismarck Archipelago.

children.name: Rhipidura phoenicura
children.description: A rufous-tailed fantail endemic to the montane forests of Java.

children.name: Rhipidura rennelliana
children.description: A fantail endemic to Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Rhipidura semirubra
children.description: A fantail endemic to Manus Island in the Bismarck Archipelago.

children.name: Rhipidura spilodera
children.description: A streaked fantail found in New Caledonia and Vanuatu.

children.name: Rhipidura superflua
children.description: A fantail endemic to the island of Buru in the Moluccas.

children.name: Rhipidura teysmanni
children.description: A fantail endemic to Sulawesi, Indonesia.

children.name: Rhipidura thysanolaema
children.description: A sooty fantail found in the montane forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Rhipidura tricolor
children.description: A small grey fantail with white eyebrow, found across Australia, New Caledonia and the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Rhipidura verheijeni
children.description: A fantail endemic to the Bismarck Archipelago.
